texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As global warming has become a major concern in recent years, various options have been investigated to reduce anthropogenic carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from various stationary sources including power and industrial plants. In particular, the iron and steel plants account for the largest CO2 emission in the global industry sector (2.3 GtonCO2/yr), accounting for 7.9% of the global CO2 emissions and 31% of the industrial CO2 emissions in 2008 (IEA and UNIDO, 2011). Consequently, although the largest interest in the implementation of CO2 reduction technologies has gone to power plants, the iron and steel industry has also drawn signi fi cant interest.
texts.text: As global warming has become a major concern in recent years, various options have been investigated to reduce anthropogenic carbon dioxide (CO2) emissions from various stationary sources including power and industrial plants. In particular, the iron and steel plants account for the largest CO2 emission in the global industry sector (2.3 GtonCO2/yr), accounting for 7.9% of the global CO2 emissions and 31% of the industrial CO2 emissions in 2008 (IEA and UNIDO, 2011). Consequently, although the largest interest in the implementation of CO2 reduction technologies has gone to power plants, the iron and steel industry has also drawn signi fi cant interest.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One promising option to reduce CO2 emissions in the iron and steel industry is the carbon capture and storage (CCS) technology (IEA and UNIDO, 2011). CCS intends to capture CO2 from fl ue gases and store it in suitable strata or sea fl oor to avoid its emission to the atmosphere. In iron and steel plants, the blast furnace (BF) is the main unit responsible for CO2 emission. Approximately two-thirds of the total CO2 emission from the iron and steel plants are generated from the BF units in the form of blast furnace gas (BFG) (Birat, 2010). Accordingly, the application of CCS technologies to the BF units could lead to a signi fi cant reduction of CO2 emissions.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A B S T R A C T
texts.text: A B S T R A C T

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An option considered for reducing carbon emissions in the iron/steel industry is the use of Top-gas recycle blast furnace (TGR-BF), which captures CO2 from the blast furnace gas (BFG) and recycles the remaining CO and H2 back to the blast furnace (BF). This study suggests four di ff erent designs of CO2 capture processes suitable for the TGR-BF application: Amine scrubbing, membrane separation, and a hybrid between the amine scrubbing and the membrane separation with and without heat recovery. In particular, the hybrid-with-heat-recovery design recovers the combustion heat of the impurities (e.g., CO and H2) in the captured CO2 stream. The four alternative processes are simulated and evaluated on the basis of multiple criteria including operating cost, capital cost, rate of CO2 avoided, and CO2 avoidance cost with various electricity sources. Furthermore, sensitivity analysis is performed by perturbing the utility prices. Among the four alternatives, the hybrid-with-heat-recovery design is shown to give the best performance with the avoidance cost of $30.4/tonCO2, outperforming the mature technologies.
texts.text: An option considered for reducing carbon emissions in the iron/steel industry is the use of Top-gas recycle blast furnace (TGR-BF), which captures CO2 from the blast furnace gas (BFG) and recycles the remaining CO and H2 back to the blast furnace (BF). This study suggests four di ff erent designs of CO2 capture processes suitable for the TGR-BF application: Amine scrubbing, membrane separation, and a hybrid between the amine scrubbing and the membrane separation with and without heat recovery. In particular, the hybrid-with-heat-recovery design recovers the combustion heat of the impurities (e.g., CO and H2) in the captured CO2 stream. The four alternative processes are simulated and evaluated on the basis of multiple criteria including operating cost, capital cost, rate of CO2 avoided, and CO2 avoidance cost with various electricity sources. Furthermore, sensitivity analysis is performed by perturbing the utility prices. Among the four alternatives, the hybrid-with-heat-recovery design is shown to give the best performance with the avoidance cost of $30.4/tonCO2, outperforming the mature technologies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Amine-based scrubbing of CO2 is a well-known technology for gas sweetening and is considered one of the most attractive CCS options due to its process simplicity and maturity. Industrial-scale amine scrubbing plants with such as those using monotehanolamine (MEA) and piperazine (PZ) as solvents, were modeled, simulated, and optimized (Abu-Zahra et al., 2007; Hasan et al., 2012, 2015; Lawal et al., 2012), mostly for CO2 removal at coal- fi red power plants. MEA (DOE and NETL, 2010; Stec et al., 2016) and PZ (Chen et al., 2013) based amine scrubbing plants have been demonstrated with pilot-scale plants for post-combustion CO2 capture at coal- fi red power plants. In addition, several projects attempted to implement this technology to the BFG and other steelmaking o ff -gas streams. The POSCO RIST program built a pilot plant that captures CO2 from the BFG via ammonia-based chemical absorption, which resulted in 5 GJ/tonCO2 of the regeneration heat duty (Kim et al., 2013). The ULCOS program tested activated methyldiethanolamine (aMDEA) based amine scrubbing on the top gas recycling blast furnace (TGR-BF), which resulted in 3.2 GJ/tonCO2 of the regeneration energy (Birat, 2010). The Japanese COURSE 50 program reported that various secondary and tertiary amines tested in pilot-scale facilities exhibited the regeneration energy as low as 2.7 GJ/ tonCO2 (Tonomura, 2013). However, these fi gures are still considered too high for the technologies to be used on a routine basis.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various ideas have been proposed to reduce the regeneration energy in amine scrubbing-based CO2 capture plants, including the use of new solvents, absorber intercooling, and variations of the stripper con fi guration (Boot-Handford et al., 2014). One promising strategy focused on in this study is a synergistic combination with the membrane separation technology. Membrane separation has also been examined for CO2 capture, both pre-combustion (Scholes et al., 2010) and postcombustion (Khalilpour et al., 2015) captures, with some supporting modeling and optimization studies (Hasan et al., 2012). In addition, modeling and optimization was conducted in an industrial-scale application for a steelmaking plant (Lie et al., 2007), resulting in the optimized CO2 capture cost of € 15.0 ∼ 17.5/tonCO2. Membrane Technology & Research Inc. (MTR), in collaboration with the University of Texas at Austin ' s research team, developed a hybrid membrane-absorption CO2 capture process for the coal- fi red power plant (Freeman et al., 2014). The research team reported that hybridization of the two capture methods reduced the solvent regeneration heat duty down to 1.90 GJ/tonCO2 and the overall CO2 capture cost as well.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The goal of this study is to evaluate various design options of the hybrid amine scrubbing and the membrane-based CO2 capture plant applied to the BFG of an iron and steel plant. It is aimed to achieve lower energy consumption than the standalone application of amine scrubbing-based CO2 capture technology. In Section 2, four alternative designs for the CO2 capture plant are proposed. Cases I and II employ amine scrubbing and membrane technologies as standalone applications; they are studied as reference cases. Cases III and IV explore a hybrid design between them. In particular, Case IV allows combustion of carbon monoxide and hydrogen impurities present in the captured CO2 stream to recover the combustion heat for use in the solvent regeneration. The four alternative CO2 capture plants are designed and simulated using commercial process simulators of Aspen Plus and MATLAB, to obtain the mass and energy balance and equipment sizing information. Process simulation results are analyzed in Section 3. In Section 4, the results are then used to perform techno-economic analysis (TEA) to evaluate the four cases with respect to four criteria including the operating cost, the capital cost, the amount of CO2 avoidance, and the CO2 avoidance cost.

texts.orig: The ULCOS project investigated several CO2 capture technologies for TGR-BF. Vacuum pressure swing adsorption (VPSA) was identi fi ed as the most promising among the choices, which included amine scrubbing and PSA combined with cryogenic distillation (Birat, 2010). However, the captured CO2 of the VPSA application is not suitable for CO2 sequestration because the CO impurity level is too high (6.4 mol%). This may increase the compression energy for sequestration and also can cause safety problems in case of leakage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The purity of the captured CO2 stream should be determined since it a ff ects both CO2 capture and compression energy (Boot-Handford et al., 2014). For some CO2 capture technologies such as physical adsorption and membrane separation, high CO2 purity results in high CO2 capture cost. In the case of BF, various impurities can be found in the captured CO2 stream including N2, O2, H2O, CO, and H2. To the best of our knowledge, no robust impurity concentration criteria for CCS have been established yet. Following the standard in the literature, the minimum CO2 purity is set as 95 mol% in this study. The maximum impurity concentration levels for CO and H2O in the captured CO2 stream are speci fi ed as 0.2 mol% both. This is because CO is very toxic and may be harmful in case of leakage, and high concentration of H2O forms clathrates in a pipeline, so it can cause a process safety problem. A common criterion for water contents in a natural gas pipeline is 7 lbs/ MMcft (equivalent to 141 ppmv); the humidity can be easily eliminated by the triethylene glycol (TEG) dehydration process (Foss, 2004).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We designed four alternative processes for CO2 capture in the TGRBF application. Case I and II serve as the baseline processes in evaluating how much the hybridization reduces the CO2 capture cost. Case III and IV are the hybrid cases. Case IV is di ff erent from Case III in that it performs combustion of CO and H2 impurities resulting in heat recovery. For all the cases, the BFG is cooled down to 40 °C and all particulate matters, e.g., NOx and SOx, are assumed to be eliminated prior to capture.

Case I and II serve as the baseline processes in evaluating how much the hybridization reduces the CO2 capture cost. Case III and IV are the hybrid cases. Case IV is di ff erent from Case III in that it performs combustion of CO and H2 impurities resulting in heat recovery. For all the cases, the BFG is cooled down to 40 °C and all particulate matters, e.g., NOx and SOx, are assumed to be eliminated prior to capture.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Case I. Amine scrubbing
texts.text: Case I. Amine scrubbing

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Case I is a baseline CO2 capture plant adopting an amine scrubbing technology. Amine aqueous solution selectively absorbs CO2 at a low temperature, and desorbs it at a high temperature under thermal energy supplement. There are several key design factors a ff ecting the regeneration heat duty of the process: the type of amine, the amine concentration in the solution, and the CO2/amine loadings of the lean and rich solutions. Generally, the regeneration heat is minimized with a maximum regeneration temperature allowed by the thermal degradation rate of amine. Piperazine (PZ) is a stable form of amine which exhibits a high regeneration temperature at a moderate amine concentration (Boot-Handford et al., 2014). In this study, 8 m aqueous PZ solution is chosen given its acceptable volatility, viscosity, and solubility of PZ at the operating condition (Rochelle et al., 2011).
texts.text: Case I is a baseline CO2 capture plant adopting an amine scrubbing technology. Amine aqueous solution selectively absorbs CO2 at a low temperature, and desorbs it at a high temperature under thermal energy supplement. There are several key design factors a ff ecting the regeneration heat duty of the process: the type of amine, the amine concentration in the solution, and the CO2/amine loadings of the lean and rich solutions. Generally, the regeneration heat is minimized with a maximum regeneration temperature allowed by the thermal degradation rate of amine. Piperazine (PZ) is a stable form of amine which exhibits a high regeneration temperature at a moderate amine concentration (Boot-Handford et al., 2014). In this study, 8 m aqueous PZ solution is chosen given its acceptable volatility, viscosity, and solubility of PZ at the operating condition (Rochelle et al., 2011).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The CO2 capture plant is designed and simulated by Aspen PLUS (ver. 8.6). The electrolyte Non-Random Two Liquid (e-NRTL) model is selected for the thermodynamic calculations in the simulation. Detailed kinetic parameters of the reaction between CO2 and PZ are presented in Appendix A (Table A1). The schematics of the CO2 capture plant using the PZ solution is illustrated in Fig. 2. The plant is composed of an absorber, a stripper, and a heat exchanger. BFG is cooled down to 40 °C before being fed into the absorber. The 8 m aqueous PZ solution is supplied from the top of the absorber. Regeneration temperature can reach up to 161 °C (Boot-Handford et al., 2014), but it is set as 150 °C to reduce the degradation of PZ in the stripper. The absorber and the stripper use packing materials, and they are appropriately sized to be within 60 ∼ 80% of the fl ooding range. The detailed packed column model is explained in Appendix A. 10 °C of the minimum temperature di ff erence between the hot and cold streams of the heat exchanger is assumed. Other key design speci fi cations are listed in Table 3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Case II. Membrane separation
texts.text: Case II. Membrane separation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Case II is a baseline CO2 capture plant using a membrane separation technology. Membrane separation separates a certain component from other components by their permeability di ff erences. Drag force of gas permeation is the gas ' s partial pressure di ff erence between the two sides. For CO2 capture, a CO2 selective membrane can be used which allows for a much higher mass transfer rate of CO2 than the other gases. Membrane separation has recently attracted signi fi cant attention for CO2 capture as advanced membranes with high CO2 selectivity and permeability have been developed. Membrane Technology & Research Inc. (MTR) has developed high-end performance membranes for CO2 capture, one of which is the MTR Polaris membrane. As shown in Table A1, it boasts a permeability of 1000 GPU (gas permeation unit) for CO2 and CO2/N2 selectivity of as high as 50 (Baker et al., 2013, 2015).

Membrane separation separates a certain component from other components by their permeability di ff erences. Drag force of gas permeation is the gas ' s partial pressure di ff erence between the two sides. For CO2 capture, a CO2 selective membrane can be used which allows for a much higher mass transfer rate of CO2 than the other gases. Membrane separation has recently attracted signi fi cant attention for CO2 capture as advanced membranes with high CO2 selectivity and permeability have been developed. Membrane Technology & Research Inc. (MTR) has developed high-end performance membranes for CO2 capture, one of which is the MTR Polaris membrane. As shown in Table A1, it boasts a permeability of 1000 GPU (gas permeation unit) for CO2 and CO2/N2 selectivity of as high as 50 (Baker et al., 2013, 2015).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In spite of the high CO2/CO selectivity, it is not possible to achieve a su ffi ciently high level of CO2 purity (> 95 mol%) by a single membrane unit. Rather, three membrane units are connected in series to keep the CO concentration of the exit gas below 0.2 mol%. The retentate pressure is fi xed at the pressure of the BFG (2.5 bar). The permeate pressure and the membrane area are then adjusted to control the CO2 capture rate and purity. The detailed conditions are displayed in Fig. 3.
texts.text: In spite of the high CO2/CO selectivity, it is not possible to achieve a su ffi ciently high level of CO2 purity (> 95 mol%) by a single membrane unit. Rather, three membrane units are connected in series to keep the CO concentration of the exit gas below 0.2 mol%. The retentate pressure is fi xed at the pressure of the BFG (2.5 bar). The permeate pressure and the membrane area are then adjusted to control the CO2 capture rate and purity. The detailed conditions are displayed in Fig. 3.

texts.orig: Case IV is similar to Case III but the main di ff erence comes from the post-treatment of the captured CO2 product. In Case IV , the captured CO2 from the membrane section has a 90 mol% purity with the most impurities being CO and H2 which are fl ammable. Before the compression, the CO2 product stream is sent to a boiler as illustrated in Fig. 3(c). With a pure O2 supplement, CO and H2 are combusted to CO2 and H2O. Since the concentrations of CO and H2 are too low ( ∼ 10 mol % of CO + H2) to be combusted as is, natural gas (assumed to pure methane) is added to ensure the combustion stability. As gas combustion reaction prefers a high pressure, the CO2 product is compressed to 20 bar before being sent to the boiler. The combustion heat generated in the boiler is recovered to produce low-pressure steam, which is then supplied to the stripper in the amine scrubbing section. The membrane section takes on 62% of the total CO2 capture duty while the amine scrubbing section does the rest (Table 2). This CO2 capture load distribution is selected so that combustion energy can cover the entire steam consumption in the amine scrubbing section. This con fi guration results in a higher energy e ffi ciency than Case III . Simulation of the boiler is performed in Aspen PLUS.

texts.orig: Membrane sections consume two types of utilities: electricity for gas compression and cooling water for compressed gas chilling. As electricity cost is typically much higher than cooling water cost, the former solely determines the operating cost for the membrane separation section. Speci fi c energy consumptions in Cases II, III, and IV are not signi fi cantly di ff erent from one another (Table 4). Compression energy consumption of the membrane section in Case III (0.32 GJ/tonCO2) is lower than in Case II (0.58 GJ/tonCO2). This is probably due to a higher CO2 fraction in the outlet of the membrane section, leading to higher required permeate pressure in the fi rst membrane module and lower compression energy consumption (Fig. 3(b)). Electricity consumption levels in the compression section in Cases II and IV are lower than in Cases I and III because the CO2 product stream from the membrane section has lower pressure (1 bar) than that from the stripper which operates at pressure 11.3 ∼ 14.4 bar. As CO2 purity for the CO2 product stream decreases from Case III to Case IV , the membrane section in Case IV results in lower electricity consumption (0.141 GJ/tonCO2) than in Case III .

In Case I , the CO2 product stream only involves humidity; accordingly, no critical impurity issue arises. Membranes do not block CO permeation perfectly, so the CO2 product stream in Cases II and III contain CO impurities but they are within the CO impurity speci fi cation (< 0.2 mol%). As Case III is a hybrid case, the CO2 product stream contains both humidity and CO. Case IV solves the CO impurity issue, but the CO2 product stream contains 2.33 mol%. of O2 impurity due to the supply of excess air for combustion.

texts.orig: These criteria compare the four cases in terms of how cost-e ff ective CO2 capture is done. Criteria 1 and 3 represent changes in the operating cost and CO2 emission, with respect to the blast furnace without CCS, respectively. Criterion 3 includes indirect CO2 emission originating from the generation of electricity, steam, and other utilities. To calculate the values of these criteria, mass and energy balance of the non-CCS blast furnace is needed (Fig. 1). Modern steelmakers typically combust BFG to generate electricity using gas turbines. When a blast furnace is modi fi ed to adopt the concept of TGR-BF, it will end up sacri fi cing the electricity production from the BFG combustion. To compensate for this, the same amount of electricity should be purchased or generated. The BFG has a comparatively low heat content as the portion of fuel compounds (e.g. H2 and CO) in BFG is low (Table 1). In our study, it is assumed that natural gas of an equivalent heat content is supplied to the existing gas turbine so that the electricity generation rate remains the same. For this, no extra capital cost is added.

texts.orig: The price and CO2 emission factor of electricity are highly a ff ected by the primary energy source. In this work, four di ff erent power generation scenarios are considered: pulverized-coal (PC), natural gas combined cycle (NGCC), nuclear, and solar-photovoltaics (PV). Electricity price varies by circumstances (e.g. fuel price and tax policy), so an average value (in the U.S. in 2015) is provided in the cost estimation. Then, a sensitivity analysis is conducted by perturbing the price to see how the values of the performance criteria change. However, the CO2 emission factors of power generation depend mostly on fuel type and electricity generation e ffi ciency. So, a fi xed value is assigned to each power generation type.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Conclusion
texts.text: 5. Conclusion

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Top-gas recycling blast furnace is an attractive process option for the steelmaking industry as it can reduce CO2 emission as well as coal consumption if a carbon capture technology is applied. In this work, four CO2 capture processes employing amine (piperazine) scrubbing and/or membrane separation are designed, and their performances are evaluated in terms of economics and CO2 avoidance. The four processes involve amine scrubbing, membrane separation, and their hybridization, with and without heat recovery. In order to compare such four alternatives, various evaluation criteria are introduced such as operating cost change due to the CCS application, capital cost for the CCS plant, rate of CO2 avoided, and CO2 avoidance cost. Furthermore, sensitivity analysis is conducted with respect to utility prices and the CO2 emission factor for electricity generation. Evaluation results show that the hybridization case with heat recovery is the most competitive among the examined options as it exhibits the lowest operating cost, the highest rate of CO2 avoided, and the lowest CO2 avoidance cost. The main reason arises from the fact that the combustible impurities in the captured CO2 stream are converted into thermal energy, which supplements the reboiler heat duty of the amine scrubbing plant.

In this work, four CO2 capture processes employing amine (piperazine) scrubbing and/or membrane separation are designed, and their performances are evaluated in terms of economics and CO2 avoidance. The four processes involve amine scrubbing, membrane separation, and their hybridization, with and without heat recovery. In order to compare such four alternatives, various evaluation criteria are introduced such as operating cost change due to the CCS application, capital cost for the CCS plant, rate of CO2 avoided, and CO2 avoidance cost. Furthermore, sensitivity analysis is conducted with respect to utility prices and the CO2 emission factor for electricity generation. Evaluation results show that the hybridization case with heat recovery is the most competitive among the examined options as it exhibits the lowest operating cost, the highest rate of CO2 avoided, and the lowest CO2 avoidance cost. The main reason arises from the fact that the combustible impurities in the captured CO2 stream are converted into thermal energy, which supplements the reboiler heat duty of the amine scrubbing plant.

texts.orig: A2. Packed bed models of the absorber and stripper
texts.text: A2. Packed bed models of the absorber and stripper

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Packed columns are used for the absorber and the stripper, fl ash drums are adapted to the amine scrubber and the condenser above stripper, and a kettle type reboiler is selected to the reboiler. The packing materials of the absorber and the stripper are Norton IMTP of 1.5-in type and the same of 3-in type, respectively. It is assumed that equilibrium is achieved in the amine scrubber, condenser, and reboiler. For the absorber and stripper, the RadFrac ® rate-based model is selected to consider the mass transfer resistance between the liquid phase and the vapor phase. In both the absorber and stripper, the reaction kinetics are included for a rate-based model. Packing height of the absorber and the stripper is fi xed at 20 m and 5m, respectively. The rate-based packed beds were discretized to one stage per one meter height, and accordingly, the absorber and the stripper are discretized to 20 stages and 5 stages each. As mentioned in t Section 2.3, their diameters are adjusted within 60 ∼ 80% fl ooding constraints. Calculated diameters for each case are listed in Table 3.

In both the absorber and stripper, the reaction kinetics are included for a rate-based model. Packing height of the absorber and the stripper is fi xed at 20 m and 5m, respectively. The rate-based packed beds were discretized to one stage per one meter height, and accordingly, the absorber and the stripper are discretized to 20 stages and 5 stages each. As mentioned in t Section 2.3, their diameters are adjusted within 60 ∼ 80% fl ooding constraints. Calculated diameters for each case are listed in Table 3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In order to deal mass transfer resistance, liquid and vapor fi lms are constructed in the packed bed simulation. As only di ff usion matters in the vapor phase in both the absorber and the stripper, mass transfer resistance within vapor phase was simulated using a single fi lm assumption. On the other hand, transport phenomena in the liquid phase are quite di ff erent. This di ff erence is because the reactions discussed in Section A1 are involved in the liquid phase. In order to calculate the mass transfer rate accompanied by the fi lm reaction, the liquid phase fi lm is discretized into fi ve sections. Film thickness is not speci fi ed and instead is calculated by Aspen Plus, including the discretization ratio. Mass transfer coe ffi cients between liquid phase and vapor phase for random packing are correlated by referring to Onda et al. (Onda et al., 1968). Assumed surface area per unit volume for the packing is 151 m 2 /m 3 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The absorber column is operated under an adiabatic condition while the stripper temperature is controlled by the reboiler and the condenser. As the CO2 absorption reaction with piperazine is quite exothermic ( ∼ 70 kJ/gmol CO2) (Rochelle et al., 2011), the column temperature slightly increases from the original temperatures of lean solvent and CO2 inlet gas. Simulated CO2/PZ molar loading of all amine scrubbing processes in Cases I, III, and IV was high (0.55 ∼ 0.82, Table 3). This is probably due to the high CO2 partial pressure within the absorber gas phase: 0.5 ∼ 0.9 bar for the inlet and 0.1 bar for the outlet. The simulated absorber temperature and liquid phase mass transfer coe ffi cient pro fi les for Cases I, III, and IV are presented in the Supplementary material (Supplementary Fig. 3 and 4) for readers who are interested in the detailed results. In summary, the simulated absorption temperature range for all three cases was 40 ∼ 62 °C, which coincides with the typical amine absorption temperature range. The estimated liquid phase mass transfer coe ffi cient ( k g ' ) was roughly 1 ∼ 2×10 -6 mol/m 2 s Pa, which also corresponds to the known k g ' range (Dugas and Rochelle, 2011).
